chapters.title: The obsession with being productive has made it difficult for people to appreciate family time
chapters.text:
The human lifespan is somewhat short, and the average length of time for most people is a little over 4,000 weeks. Time is one of our most valuable commodities, yet the notion that we should be more productive, more efficient, or devote our time to something else haunts many of us today.

Life hacks and time management tactics don’t help us become more productive; they only make us more anxious and depleted.

There is little chance that we will be able to spend all of our 4000 weeks of life doing exactly what we desire. The world prioritizes productivity over everything else, including our happiness. Although there’s nothing wrong with trying to make a living or a name for ourselves, we have placed too much emphasis on maximizing our time that we burden ourselves with unnecessary expectations.

Believing that we have to be special beings in a unique world is why many of us lose sight of living a normal life without feeling bad about it. Since we are obsessed with accumulating wealth and notoriety, people have found it difficult to appreciate the almost 4,000 weeks we have left on this planet. We spend our free time doing constructive things like thinking or working instead of relaxing and enjoying life. In this summary, we'll discover fresh views on time and how to manage it more effectively, thus challenging the status quo and encouraging us to embrace the beauty of change.

chapters.title: The best way to make the most of our time is to spend it doing the things we love
chapters.text:
Our current mindset about time is so ingrained in us that it might be challenging to remember that there was once a period when it was not always like this. We have come to conceive time as something that functions independently of us since the creation of clocks, so we feel bad if we don't spend our time on activities that we find significant.

Before the invention of clocks, time was just the channel through which existence unfolded. Workers used to get up with the sun and go to bed at nightfall, with the length of their days shifting with the seasons; there was no need to imagine time as something different from existence. When time and life got separated, time became a thing we employed. This transition is accountable for our contemporary challenges and fears over time.

To live as though every day is our last can be absurd, but we can only connect with life if we accept that our time is finite.

There will always be important things we should do but which we’ll never be able to. We will never be able to create time for everything that seems essential or even enough of it because the more we finish things, the more stuff worth doing we discover. This is why we could perhaps quit believing there is a future when we have checked all the boxes and life is flawless.

In a society awash with many inspiring activities, wanting always to do more things may overwhelm us. So, when we stop thinking that squeezing more work into our days would bring us tranquility, it becomes easier to find comfort in the middle of enormous expectations. Moreover, since our peace of mind no longer relies on our ability to satisfy our obligations, we free ourselves of some unnecessary burdens.

Arguably, time management is all life is. Yet the modern discipline known as time management — like its hipper cousin, productivity — is a depressingly narrow-minded affair, focused on how to crank through as many work tasks as possible, or on devising the perfect morning routine, or on cooking all your dinners for the week in one big batch on Sundays. ~ Oliver Burkeman

chapters.title: Procrastination is a natural and healthy human behavior
chapters.text:
Many of us recognize the tendency to postpone our duties, but procrastination is a natural human inclination that we cannot avoid. Therefore, we must shift our attention from striving to get everything done and prioritizing what is most important to becoming better procrastinators. The three principles of becoming a better procrastinator are:

Pay yourself first

Originally a financial concept, we can use it for time management. Instead of waiting for a free period, we can create time in our calendar or focus on it during the first hour after we get up. If we have a big project, we may have to pay ourselves first, especially if we can't see how we'll fit it into our schedule.

Limit your work in progress

This means we can set a cap for how many projects we can undertake at once. We can do this by realizing that the amount of work we can do in one day is limited. This way, we can focus on a few essential tasks that we need to do well instead.

It's tempting to start many tasks at once while considering our options. When one of our efforts grows tough or dull, we're more inclined to move on to the next, making it harder to accomplish what we care about. Working on one thing at a time requires breaking it down into manageable chunks. We should simply work on a few things and achieve our objectives on any given day.

Limit your mid-level priorities

Burkeman recounts Warren Buffett imparting wisdom to his pilot. The billionaire instructed him to compile a list of the top 25 things he desired in life and then organize them in order of importance. Warren Buffet then told his pilot that the top five are the priorities for which he should plan his time.

When our peace of mind isn't dependent on our capacity to meet our wants, it's easier to cope with excessive expectations.

We simply do not have enough time to achieve all of our objectives. So, we must learn to say no to some of the things we want to accomplish, whether it's a romantic or work opportunity. Similarly, if an activity is not one of our top five life objectives, we should avoid it.

chapters.title: Distraction is essential to our existence; hence, gaining perfect control over our attention is problematic
chapters.text:
Our odds of enjoying 4,000 weeks are modest even if we live to an average age of 80 years because life is full of surprises, and we tend to become distracted.

Involuntary actions are crucial to our survival, and that's why we can get out of the path of an oncoming vehicle. However, we must first learn to concentrate our attention to achieve our objectives. The most evident distractions we confront today are those brought about by digital technology. The fact that tech corporations benefit by collecting and monitoring our attention and selling our data for advertising is well-known. However, the threat of technology isn't limited to the fact that it consumes our free time; social media also distorts our world perspectives which is a problem.

The risks we confront, the issues we care about, and the people we disagree with are all influenced by technology, affecting our offline behavior. Burkeman was a frequent Twitter user until he noticed that technology was still draining his energy even after he had signed off for the day. Instead of savoring the time he had with his newborn child, he would contemplate the best way to express his lovely behavior in a tweet.

Living for the present and ourselves rather than striving to exist for others is an important part of enjoying a successful life.

The problem with distraction isn't just technology's fault but a combination of factors. The truth is that doing what matters to us can cause unexpected discomfort. If we must confront our limits when engaged in a necessary activity, doing something we like might be uncomfortable. We avoid facing the fact that we may not complete a creative activity and then surrender to distractions. We can work through our discomfort instead of succumbing to every irrational inclination if we become conscious of this problem.

chapters.title: Although we recognize that life is frequently beyond our control, many people waste their time attempting to arrange every minute
chapters.text:
Douglas Hofstadter, a cognitive scientist, discovered a phenomenon known as Hofstadter's law. If we attempt to allow ourselves additional time to account for procrastinating, we'll still end up going over our new anticipated time, according to this law. No matter how much planning we do, there's no assurance that things will come out the way we want them to. Attempting to manage the future adds to our stress by causing us to get preoccupied with what happens next. Yet, this isn't the only way we think about the future. Many of us are prone to the when-I-finally perspective, as described by Burkeman. We persuade ourselves that life will begin when we find the proper significant other, address our mental health concerns, or manage our workload. And even though we should be doing ourselves a favor by aiming to live in the present moment rather than living for the future, our current moment is continually straining toward some idealized future state.

While we may not fully appreciate the present moment, we can accept that we are always living in it, as it is all that exists.

Our growing tendency to occupy our leisure time with constructive activities has become an issue that we dismiss. We do various things to pass the time, but regrettably, many of us do not take the time to rest.

Mortality makes it impossible to ignore the absurdity of living solely for the future. ~ Oliver Burkeman

Having a hobby these days might be a little awkward. We tend to assume that calling anything a hobby implies that we're amateurs and that it's far more popular to cram in something else or a profit-generating business. However, having a pastime may help our lives precisely because we do it for its joy.

Hobbies also enhance our lives and make us feel more comfortable, particularly among others. For example, in a 2013 research in Sweden, Professor Terry Hartig discovered that antidepressant sales dropped at a higher rate when more individuals were on vacation. In other words, Swedish people were happiest when they could share their vacation time with others.

chapters.title: Paying attention to how we spend our days is the most effective method for choosing what to accomplish with our time
chapters.text:
When the coronavirus pandemic forced governments worldwide to enact lockdowns, there was universal consensus in the United States that, in addition to the misery and loss, the pandemic was a turning point that allowed us to pause and reassess our priorities. Even the understaffed healthcare institutions and racial disparities highlighted by the epidemic added to the sense that we were being reminded of what is really important.

Accepting our insignificance frees us from our unrealistic aspirations of making our time on Earth exceptional.

The issue with determining what matters most is that it may become an excessively tiring process, given that every one of us lives a unique reality. For instance, if someone is unable to quit their job, they may believe that having a meaningful life is out of the question since their existence depends on it. However, what we accomplish with our time is meaningless in the eyes of the cosmos.

We have physiologically programmed ourselves to experience the cosmos from our biased point of view. This serves as a motivation for us to reproduce and pass on our genes. However, our individual lives are trivial in the broad sweep of global time. This may seem to be a frightening concept at first; however, it is also very freeing with reflection. According to cosmic insignificance therapy, we are insignificant in the larger scheme of things. It makes no difference whether we lack the ability of Mozart or Albert Einstein; in the perspective of the cosmos, doing whatever makes us happy is a worthwhile way to spend our 4,000 weeks.

Did you know? The Health and Safety Executive, a UK health enforcement agency, estimates that in 2020/21, there were 822,000 instances of work-related stress, depression, or anxiety, a prevalence rate of 2,480 per 100,000 employees in the UK.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Time has devolved into a societal construct rather than a naturally occurring condition of life. We now see it as the standard by which we must live instead of a clear signal of the reality that we have a finite amount of time to live. Time is an infinite continuum that will always outlive us regardless of our actions, so we should not feel confined by it. As noted in the chapters above, although striving to be creative and unique is admirable, we lose sight of the things and people around us when it becomes our primary goal. There is a growing divide between work and personal connections as individuals spend more time working than appreciating life's beauties. According to a 2020 US Bureau of Labor Statistics study, 42% of Americans worked an average of 7.7 hours each day. With an average sleep time of approximately eight hours, the World Health Organization estimates that the typical American has around 10 hours to rest. However, most Americans spend these hours working side jobs or doing something else.

We’ve been granted the mental capacities to make almost infinitely ambitious plans, yet practically no time at all to put them into action. ~ Oliver Burkeman

Modern thought about time is a fruitless effort to manage, but it's possible to free ourselves from this sociological context. By embracing our mortality and cultivating a meaningful existence, we can work with, rather than against, human limitations such as procrastination, distraction, and the capacity to live in the present moment.

Try this

Break down your duties into tiny sections and work on them in order of priority. You may also employ technology that was created with a specific purpose in mind. For example, instead of reading on your phone, read books on an e-reader; you'll be far less likely to get distracted.

Read it till the end?
